title: Polynomial Ergodic Theorems for Strongly Mixing Commuting Transformations
speaker: Rigoberto Zelada
abstract: The goal of this talk is to present new results dealing with the asymptotic joint independence properties of commuting strongly mixing transformations along polynomials. These results form natural strongly mixing counterparts to various weakly and mildly mixing  polynomial ergodic theorems. A decisive role in the proofs is played by a new notion of convergence that is adequate for dealing with strong mixing and, as we will see, cannot be avoided while working with commuting polynomial actions. This talk is based on joint work with Vitaly Bergelson.

proof_stages.label: Weak-mixing and uniform-density background
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines convergence in uniform density, explains sparse exceptional sets, and recalls several weakly mixing polynomial ergodic theorems whose limiting correlations are independent outside a zero-density set of times.

proof_stages.label: Sigma-star limits as the strong-mixing convergence mode
proof_stages.summary: Strong-mixing counterexamples show that uniform-density limits and low-order finite-sum limits are too strong, motivating the definition of σ_l-star limits and the principle that more summands give weaker but obstruction-avoiding convergence.

proof_stages.label: Adequacy and sharpness of sigma-star convergence
proof_stages.summary: The speaker proves the mode is still sensitive by characterizing strong mixing through σ_l-star independence, comparing it with uniform-density convergence, and giving a sharp single-polynomial mixing threshold.

proof_stages.label: Strong-mixing polynomial ergodic theorems
proof_stages.summary: The main results are stated as strong-mixing counterparts of the earlier weak-mixing theorems: for suitable polynomial families and commuting transformations satisfying the corresponding strong-mixing hypotheses, σ_m-star correlations converge to the product of measures for some m depending only on the polynomials.

proof_stages.label: Van der Corput mechanism for the easier theorems
proof_stages.summary: For the analogues whose bookkeeping follows the weak-mixing PET proofs, a Hilbert-space van der Corput criterion adapted to σ-star limits gives convergence to zero, with each application costing one finite-sum parameter.

proof_stages.label: Hard equal-polynomial case and untwining
proof_stages.summary: In the difficult case with equal polynomial iterates, repeated PET/van der Corput reductions first make the n-dependence linear but leave intertwined transformations, so further van der Corput steps in auxiliary parameters are used to isolate only the strongly mixing directions such as T and TS^{-1}.

proof_stages.label: Algebraic induction controlling general PET reductions
proof_stages.summary: A polynomial leading-coefficient lemma describes how van der Corput changes the algebraic data, allowing the proof to propagate good mixing properties through all potential PET expressions without explicitly computing the enormous iterated expansion.

proof_stages.label: Ultrafilter reformulation and extensions
proof_stages.summary: Because the method is algebraic and only uses weak convergence along suitable filters, the results can be reformulated with sums of ultrafilters and adapted uniformly to strong, mild, and weak mixing by choosing the corresponding classes of ultrafilters.

examples.name: Powers-of-two sparse exception set
examples.note: The sequence supported on the zero-density set {2^k} illustrates that uniform-density convergence can ignore very sparse exceptional times, without supplying any later proof mechanism.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker defines the indicator sequence of powers of two and checks its uniform-density limit is 0 because the exception set is sparse.

examples.name: Identity-time total dependence

examples.engagement_reason: The discussion computes the identity-time/zero-shift case by noting the correlation collapses to 5(A) rather than the independent product 5(A)^{l+1}.

examples.name: Rokhlin-tower disjoint translates
examples.note: The speaker replies that dependence can also appear as vanishing intersection by shifting different levels of a Rokhlin tower, illustrating that non-independence need not mean positive clustering.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses a Rokhlin tower concretely, translating levels by one and three and observing that the resulting intersection is empty.

passes.reason: Disjoint Rokhlin-tower translates have empty intersection, giving the minimal possible correlation as an obstruction to independence.
The Rokhlin-tower translates are arranged with empty, hence minimal, intersection to show correlations can be zero rather than independent.
Disjoint Rokhlin-tower levels give the minimum possible intersection, used to show correlations can fail independence in the opposite, zero-overlap direction.
